content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 25, 1997

Russell Steele
12116 Horseshoe Lane
Nevada City, California 95959

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-317

Dear Mr. Steele:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May you participate in the NCTC's decision whether to allocate $5,000 to WNCTMA to
defray their administrative costs?

CONCLUSION

You may participate in the NCTC's decision whether to allocate $5,000 to WNCTMA to
defray their administrative costs since it does not appear that the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable or material financial effect on your economic interests.

FACTS

You are a commissioner on the Nevada County Transportation Commission (NCTC).
You are also president of the Nevada County Community Network (NCN), a non-profit
corporation. You receive a salary for this position. NCN has an ongoing partnership (as
described below) with the Grass Valley Telebusiness Center (provides remote work site for
telecommuting, office services by the hour and equipment for professional skill building) which
is managed by the Western Nevada County Transportation Management Association
(WNCTMA). The WNCTMA is a public private partnership, non-profit corporation, dedicated
to improving air quality and reducing traffic congestion by promoting alternatives to driving

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800 SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-317
Page 2

single occupancy vehicles to work.

The NCTC has some funding oversight over the WNCTMA, providing $5,000 for
administrative support and passing through a $50,000 Caltrans grant awarded to the WNCTMA.
In the past, Caltrans has administered the expenditure of these grants. However, in this instance
Caltrans used the NCTC.

The NCCN/Telebusiness Center partnership evolved over time and was not formalized in
writing. In September 1995, NCCN became a tenant in the Telebusiness Center at 640 East
Main Street, Grass Valley, CA. NCCN installed phone lines, telecommunication servers and
published the Telebusiness Center as its business address in September 1995. By March 1996,
NCCN outgrew the facility and moved its administrative offices to 848 Gold Flat Road, Nevada
City, CA. NCCN's computers and telecommunication server remain at the Telebusiness Center.

At the present time, NCCN provides internet services to the Telebusiness Center in
exchange for 100 square feet of office space equipped with power, cooling, and security. The
estimated cost of this office space is $75.00 a month. In exchange, NCCN provides WNCTMA
internet access for ten workstations. The estimated cost is $120.00 per month for ten
workstations.

You submitted your resignation to the NCCN Board of Directors in March 1997, to be
effective August 15, 1997, for health reasons. You plan to remain a commissioner on the NCTC.
In the coming fiscal year, the only anticipated action by the NCTC possibly affecting NCCN or
WNCTMA will be to allocate $5,000 to WNCTMA to defray administrative costs.

ANALYSIS

Introduction

The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose of the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to insure that public officials would
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.

Economic Interests

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate

File No. A-97-317
Page 3

family or on, among other things:² any source of income aggregating $250 or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made. NCCN is a source of income to you and will be a source of income
until 12 months after your resignation is effective, i.e., August 15, 1998. Therefore, if there is a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on NCCN resulting from your decision
whether to allocate $5,000 to WNCTMA, you may not participate in that decision.

Foreseeability

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

You have written for advice regarding whether you may participate in a governmental
decision to allocate $5,000 to defray administrative costs to WNCTMA. You have an economic
interest in NCCN. WNCTMA is a source of income to NCCN since it provides NCCN with
office space worth $75.00 a month. However, in return for this office space NCCN provides
WNCTMA internet services worth $125.00. This is the only financial connection between
WNCTMA and NCCN. Barring any additional facts, the decision whether to allocate $5,000 to
WNCTMA to defray administrative costs does not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on NCCN. Therefore, you may participate in the decision at issue.

Materiality

Even assuming foreseeability, disqualification is still only required where the foreseeable
effect on the public official's economic interest is material. The Commission has adopted a
series of regulations that provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial effects
of a decision are material. (Regulation 18702.) The standard of materiality differs depending on

² Section 87103 provides that a public official has an economic interest in any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. However, the
Act limits the definition of "business entity" to any organization or enterprise operated for profit. (Section 82005.)

File No. A-97-317
Page 4

the type of economic interest involved and whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in a decision.³

NCCN is indirectly involved in the decision at issue. Therefore, Regulation 18702.5 applies to relevant standards of materiality (copy enclosed). The standard depends on the size of the business entity involved. Your facts do not provide the size of NCCN. However, assuming the lowest standards for finding materiality, the decision would still not meet the standard since it does not appear that the decision to allocate $5,000 to WNCTMA will 1) result in an increase or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or 2) result in NCCN incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or 3) result in an increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more. Therefore, even assuming foreseeability, you may participate in the decision at issue since the materiality standard is not met.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Marte Castaños
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:MC:jlw

3        "A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or:
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity." (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

defray their administrative costs?

CONCLUSION: You may participate in the NCTC’s decision whether to allocate $5,000 to WNCTMA to
defray their administrative costs since it does not appear that the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable or material financial effect on your economic interests.
